volumeTitle: The Woman of Andros. The Self-Tormentor. The Eunuch
label: The Woman of Andros (Section front.cast - Section 1.1.135)

sections.label: Section front.cast
sections.original:
FABULAE INTERLOCUTORES .

BYRRHIA , servus . CHARINTUS , adolescens . CHREMES , senex . CRITO , hospes . DAVUS , servus . DROMO , lorarius . GLYCERIUM , meretrix . LESBIA , obstetrix . MYSIS , ancilla . PAMPHILUS , adolescens . SIMO , senex . SOSIA , libertus .
sections.translation: Dramatis Personae SIMO , [*] an aged Athenian . PAMPHILUS , [*] son of Simo . SOSIA , [*] freedman of Simo . CHREMES , [*] an aged Athenian . CHARINUS , [*] a young Athenian , in love with Philumena . CRITO , [*] a native of Andros . DAVUS , [*] servant of Simo . DROMO , [*] servant of Simo . BYRRHIA , [*] servant of Charinus . GLYCERIUM , [*] a young woman beloved by Pamphilus . MYSIS , [*] her maid-servant . LESBIA [*] a midwife .

sections.label: Section front.production
sections.original: ANDRIA . ACTA LUDIS MEGALENSIB M . FULVIO ET M . GLABRIONE AEDILIB . CURULIB . EGERUNT L . AMBIVIUS TURPIO , L . ATTILIUS PRAENESTINUS . MODOS FECIT FLACCUS CLAUDII FILIUS , TIBIIS PARIBUS DEXTRIS ET SINISTRIS . ET EST TOTA GRAECA . EDITA M . MARCELLO , CN . SULPITIO COSS .
sections.translation:
THE TITLE OF THE PLAY .
PERFORMED at the Megalensian Games ; [*] M . Fulvius and M . Glabrio being Curule Aediles . [*] Ambivius Turpio and Lucius Atilius Praenestinus [*] performed it . Flaccus , the freedman of Claudius , [*] composed the music , to a pair of treble flutes and bass flutes [*] alternately . And it is entirely Grecian . [*] Published — M . Marcellus and Cneius Sulpicius being Consuls . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.1
sections.original: Poeta quum primum animum ad scribendum appulit ,
sections.translation: THE POET , when first he applied his mind to writing , thought that the only duty which devolved on him was , that the Plays he should compose might please the public . But he perceives that it has fallen out entirely otherwise ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.5
sections.original: Nam in prologis scribundis operam abutitur ,
sections.translation: for he is wasting his labor in writing Prologues , not for the purpose of relating the plot , but to answer the slanders of a malevolent old Poet . [*] Now I beseech you , give your attention to the thing which they impute as a fault . Menander composed the Andrian [*] and the Perinthian . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.10
sections.original: Qui utramvis recte norit ambas noverit .
sections.translation: He who knows either of them well , will know them both ; they are in plot not very different , and yet they have been composed in different language and style . What suited , he confesses he has transferred into the Andrian from the Perinthian , and has employed them as his own .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.15
sections.original: Id isti vituperant factum ; atque in eo disputant ,
sections.translation: These parties censure this proceeding ; and on this point they differ from him , that Plays ought not to be mixed up together . By being thus knowing , do they not show that they know nothing at all ? For while they are censuring him , they are censuring Naevius , Plautus , and Ennius , [*] whom our Poet has for his precedents ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.20
sections.translation: whose carelessness he prefers to emulate , rather than the mystifying carefulness [*] of those parties . Therefore , I advise them to be quiet in future , and to cease to slander ; that they may not be made acquainted with their own misdeeds . Be well disposed , then ; attend with unbiased mind , and consider the matter ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.25
sections.original: Ut pernoscatis , ecquid spei sit reliquum ,
sections.translation: that you may determine what hope is left ; whether the Plays which he shall in future compose anew , are to be witnessed , or are rather to be driven off the stage .

sections.label: Section 1.1.28
sections.original: Vos istaec intro auferte : abite . Sosia ,
sections.translation: Do you carry those things away in-doors ; begone . ( Beckoning to SOSIA .) Sosia , just step here ; I want a few words with you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.29b
sections.translation: Consider it as said ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.translation: that these things are to be taken care of , I suppose . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.1.30b
sections.translation: No , it ’ s another matter .

sections.label: Section 1.1.30c
sections.translation: What is there that my ability can effect for you more than this ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.32
sections.original: Nihil istac opus est arte ad hanc rem quam paro :
sections.translation: There ’ s no need of that ability in the matter which I have in hand ; but of those qualities which I have ever known as existing in you , fidelity and secrecy .

sections.label: Section 1.1.34b
sections.translation: I await your will .

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: Ego postquam te emi , a parvulo ut semper tibi
sections.translation: Since I purchased you , you know that , from a little child , your servitude with me has always been easy and light . From a slave I made you my freedman ; [*] for this reason , because you served me with readiness . The greatest recompense that I possessed , I bestowed upon you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.translation: I bear it in mind .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40b
sections.translation: I am not changed .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40c
sections.translation: If I have done or am doing aught that is pleasing to you , Simo , I am glad that it has been done ; and that the same has been gratifying to you , I consider sufficient thanks . But this is a cause of uneasiness to me ; for the recital is , as it were , a censure [*] to one forgetful of a kindness .

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: Quin tu uno verbo dic quid est quod me velis .
sections.translation: But tell me , in one word , what it is that you want with me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.46
sections.original: Ita faciam : hoc primum in hac re praedico tibi ;
sections.translation: I ’ ll do so . In the first place , in this affair I give you notice : this , which you suppose to be such , is not a real marriage .

sections.label: Section 1.1.48
sections.translation: Why do you pretend it then ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.48b
sections.translation: You shall hear all the matter from the beginning ; by that means you ’ ll be acquainted with both my son ’ s mode of life and my own design ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Cognosces , et quid facere in hac re te velim .
sections.translation: and what I want you to do in this affair . For after he had passed youthfulness , [*] Sosia , and had obtained free scope of living , ( for before , how could you know or understand his disposition , while youthful age , fear , and a master [*] were checking him ?)—

sections.label: Section 1.1.54b
sections.translation: That ’ s true .

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.translation: What all young men , for the most part , do ,— devote their attention to some particular pursuit , either to training horses or dogs for hunting , or to the philosophers ; [*] in not one of these did he engage in particular beyond the rest , and yet in all of them in a moderate degree .

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.translation: I was pleased .

sections.label: Section 1.1.60b
sections.translation: Not without reason ; for this I deem in life to be especially advantageous ; that one do nothing to excess . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.1.62
sections.original: Sic vita erat : facile omnes perferre ac pati
sections.translation: Such was his mode of life ; readily to bear and to comply with all ; with whomsoever he was in company , to them to resign himself ; to devote himself to their pursuits ; at variance with no one ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: Nunquam praeponens se illis : ita facillime
sections.translation: never preferring himself to them . Thus most readily you may acquire praise without envy , and gain friends .

sections.label: Section 1.1.67
sections.original: Sapienter vitam instituit : namque hoc tempore
sections.translation: He has wisely laid down his rule of life ; for in these days obsequiousness begets friends ; sincerity , dislike .

sections.label: Section 1.1.69
sections.translation: Meanwhile , three years ago , [*] a certain woman

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.translation: from Andros removed hither into this neighborhood , driven by poverty and the neglect of her relations , of surpassing beauty and in the bloom of youth .

sections.label: Section 1.1.73
sections.original: Hei vereor ne quid Andria apportet mali .
sections.translation: Ah ! I ’ m afraid that this Andrian will bring some mischief .

sections.label: Section 1.1.74
sections.original: Primum haec pudice vitam parce ac duriter
sections.translation: At first , in a modest way , she passed her life with thriftiness and in hardship ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: Agebat , lana ac tela victum quaeritans .
sections.translation: seeking a livelihood with her wool and loom . But after an admirer made advances , promising her a recompense , first one and then another ; as the disposition of all mankind has a downward tendency from industry toward pleasure , she accepted their proposals , and then began to trade upon her beauty .

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: Qui tum illam amabant forte , ita ut fit , filium
sections.translation: Those who then were her admirers , by chance , as it often happens , took my son thither that he might be in their company . Forthwith I said to myself , He is surely caught ; he is smitten . [*] In the morning I used to observe their servant-boys coming or going away ; I used to make inquiry , Here , my lad ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: Dic sodes , quis heri Chrysidem habuit ? Nam Andriae
sections.translation: tell me , will you , who had Chrysis yesterday ? for that was the name of the Andrian .

sections.label: Section 1.1.86b
sections.translation: I understand .

sections.label: Section 1.1.86c
sections.translation: Phaedrus , or Clinias , or Niceratus , they used to say ; for these three then loved her at the same time . Well now , what did Pamphilus do ? What ? He gave his contribution ; [*] he took part in the dinner . Just so on another day

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.translation: I made inquiry , but I discovered nothing whatever that affected Pamphilus . In fact , I thought him sufficiently proved , and a great pattern of continence ; for he who is brought into contact with dispositions of that sort , and his feelings are not aroused even under such circumstances ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: Scias posse habere iam ipsum suae vitae modum .
sections.translation: you may be sure that he is already capable of undertaking the governance of his own life . This pleased me , and every body with one voice began to say all kinds of flattering things , and to extol my good fortune , in having a son endowed with such a disposition . What need is there of talking ? Chremes , influenced by this report ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: Ultro ad me venit unicam gnatam suam
sections.translation: came to me of his own accord , to offer his only daughter as a wife to my son , with a very large portion . It pleased me ; I betrothed him ; this was the day appointed for the nuptials .

sections.label: Section 1.1.103
sections.original: Quid obstat cur non verae fiant ?
sections.translation: What then stands in the way ? Why should they not take place ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.103b
sections.translation: You shall hear . In about a few days after these things had been agreed on ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.translation: Chrysis , this neighbor , dies .

sections.label: Section 1.1.105b
sections.translation: Bravo ! You ’ ve made me happy . I was afraid for him on account of Chrysis .

sections.label: Section 1.1.106b
sections.translation: Then my son was often there , with those who had admired Chrysis ; with them he took charge of the funeral ; sorrowful , in the mean time , he sometimes wept with them in condolence . Then that pleased me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.110
sections.translation: Thus I reflected : He by reason of this slight intimacy takes her death so much to heart ; what if he himself had wooed her ? What will he do for me his father ? All these things I took to be the duties of a humane disposition and of tender feelings . Why do I detain you with many words ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.115
sections.original: Egomet quoque eius causa in funus prodeo ,
sections.translation: Even I myself , [*] for his sake , went forth to the funeral , as yet suspecting no harm .

sections.label: Section 1.1.116b
sections.translation: Ha ! what is this ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.116c
sections.translation: You shall know . She is brought out ; we proceed . In the mean time , among the females who were there present , I saw by chance one young woman of beauteous form .

sections.label: Section 1.1.119b
sections.translation: Very likely .

sections.label: Section 1.1.119c
sections.translation: And of countenance , Sosia ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.120
sections.original: Adeo modesto , adeo venusto , ut nihil supra .
sections.translation: so modest , so charming , that nothing could surpass . As she appeared to me to lament beyond the rest , and as she was of a figure handsome and genteel beyond the other women , I approached the female attendants ; [*] I inquired who she was . They said that she was the sister of Chrysis .

sections.label: Section 1.1.125
sections.original: Percussit illico animum : at at ! hoc illud est ,
sections.translation: It instantly struck my mind : Ay , ay , this is it ; hence those tears , hence that sympathy .

sections.label: Section 1.1.127
sections.translation: How I dread what you are coming to !

sections.label: Section 1.1.127b
sections.translation: The funeral procession meanwhile advances ; we follow ; we come to the burying-place . [*] She is placed upon the pile ; they weep . In the mean time , this sister ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.130
sections.original: Quam dixi ad flammam accessit imprudentius ,
sections.translation: whom I mentioned , approached the flames too incautiously , with considerable danger . There , at that moment , Pamphilus , in his extreme alarm , discovers his well-dissembled and long-hidden passion ; he runs up , clasps the damsel by the waist . My Glycerium , says he , what are you doing ? Why are you going to destroy yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.135
sections.original: Tum illa , ut consuetum facile amorem cerneres ,
sections.translation: Then she , so that you might easily recognize their habitual attachment , weeping , threw herself back upon him — how affectionately !
